one_line_takeaway: VRU is a variance-reduced first-order unlearning algorithm that provably achieves (ε,δ)-unlearning while using forget-set gradients as an active optimization signal (not just noise calibration), yielding an O(1/e) vs O(1/e²) convergence-rate improvement over the prior best certified method (NFT) and, in strongly-convex logistic-regression experiments, up to ~2 orders of magnitude lower excess risk at small forget fractions.
key_finding: In the certified-methods comparison (Figure 1), VRU achieves the lowest excess risk of all methods for rf ≤ 0.1, with the performance gap versus NFT widening as rf shrinks, reaching nearly two orders of magnitude at rf=1e-3, matching the theoretical O(1/e) vs O(1/e²) prediction. In the empirical-methods comparison (Figure 2), VRU achieves the lowest empirical privacy risk (MIA accuracy) among all tested methods at every tested rf, and the lowest excess risk for small/moderate rf; at rf=0.1 VRU has higher excess risk than the empirical baselines because it is the only method that adds calibrated noise (which scales with rf), so the authors note full retraining becomes preferable once rf ≥ 0.1. The Fine-Tune baseline shows the highest MIA privacy leakage, especially at small rf, attributed to it having no mechanism to actively degrade forget-set loss.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Variance-Reduced Unlearning (VRU), the first first-order (ε,δ)-unlearning algorithm that directly incorporates forget-set gradients into its update rule while provably satisfying formal (ε,δ)-unlearning guarantees. VRU's stochastic gradient estimator adds a correction term -(rf/(1-rf))∇ℓ(θ*, ξf) (evaluated at the pre-trained optimum θ*, using forget-set samples) to the usual retain-set gradient difference ∇ℓ(θ,ξr)-∇ℓ(θ*,ξr), inspired by SVRG variance reduction but anchored at θ* rather than requiring periodic full-batch recomputation. The algorithm runs Projected SGD with this estimator (projected onto a ball of radius (rf/(1-rf))·L/µ around θ*) followed by Gaussian noise addition to satisfy (ε,δ)-unlearning via the Gaussian mechanism.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender security paper in the adversarial-ML sense; the 'threat model' is the standard (ε,δ)-unlearning privacy definition (Ginart et al., 2019), which requires statistical indistinguishability (up to (ε,δ)) between the model produced by the unlearning algorithm and a model retrained from scratch on the retain set only, for any measurable subset S of parameter space. This bounds what an observer/adversary with access to the unlearned model could infer about the removed (forget-set) data. As an empirical privacy check (not part of the formal threat model), the paper also evaluates residual leakage using U-LiRA, an unlearning-adapted variant of the LiRA membership-inference attack (Hayes et al., 2024; Carlini et al., 2022), measuring whether an adversary can distinguish forgotten samples from unseen test samples via MIA accuracy (50% = indistinguishable/perfect unlearning).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Conclusion): (1) analysis requires strong convexity, smoothness, and Lipschitzness of the per-sample loss, which does not directly cover neural networks; (2) the theory assumes exact knowledge of the pre-trained optimum θ*, though the authors state experiments 'suggest robustness to inexact initialization' without formal guarantees for that regime; (3) bounds depend on the local condition number κℓ, which can still be restrictive in practice; (4) at larger forget fractions (rf ≥ 0.1) VRU's added noise erodes its utility advantage over empirical (non-certified) methods, and the authors explicitly note practitioners may then prefer full retraining. They flag extending VRU to relaxed convexity notions (Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, neural tangent kernel regime) as future work to better capture neural-network behavior. The empirical validation is limited to a single strongly-convex logistic regression task on one small tabular dataset (Digit/pen-based handwritten digits), not deep networks or LLM unlearning benchmarks (e.g., TOFU, MUSE, WMDP) that the introduction cites as motivating context.

datasets_used: Digit dataset (Alpaydin and Alimoglu, 1996; UCI Pen-Based Recognition of Handwritten Digits)
quantitative_results: Corollary 4.2: Te_max(VRU)/Te_max(NFT) = Θ(κℓ³ log(1/δ) · e/e0), i.e. VRU improves NFT's O(1/e²) dependence to O(1/e)

Figure 1: VRU achieves lowest excess risk for all tested rf ≤ 0.1 (5 log-spaced values in [1e-3,1e-1], 10-epoch compute budget, κ_{ε,δ}=1, averaged over 30 seeds); gap vs NFT widens to nearly ~2 orders of magnitude at rf=1e-3

Figure 2: under 5-epoch budget across rf ∈ {3e-3, 2e-2, 1e-1} (averaged over 3 runs), VRU has lowest U-LiRA MIA accuracy (empirical privacy risk) at every rf tested; VRU has lowest excess risk at small/moderate rf but higher excess risk than empirical baselines at rf=0.1 due to its noise-addition step
MIA accuracy near 50% (random guessing) for most methods across settings, attributed to reduced discriminative power of MIA in strongly-convex (non-overparameterized) settings
baselines_compared: NFT (Noise-and-Fine-Tune, Neel et al. 2021) - representative certified (ε,δ)-unlearning method
Retraining baselines: GD, SGD, SVRG (Johnson and Zhang, 2013)
SCRUB (Kurmanji et al., 2024) - empirical method alternating KL-divergence maximization on forget set / minimization on retain set
NegGrad+ (Kurmanji et al., 2024) - empirical method alternating gradient ascent on forget set and descent on retain set
Fine-Tune - empirical baseline performing only gradient descent on the retain set
